summary: Mary Midgley discusses her dissatisfaction with the hyper-analytical, language-focused philosophy of mid-20th-century Oxford, advocating instead for a holistic approach that connects philosophy to real human life and motives. She emphasizes that ethics cannot be modeled on physics or mathematics, champions accessible writing, and advises students to study philosophy alongside other subjects to avoid over-abstraction.

principles.statement: Philosophy must address life as a whole, not just small verbal ambiguities.
principles.rationale: Focusing exclusively on the meaning of specific words ignores the larger world and the real conflicts people face in their lives.
principles.source_quote: there are much bigger questions that's the point there's a much whole larger a world which they were not looking at

principles.statement: Ethics cannot be modeled on mathematics or physics.
principles.rationale: Ethics requires understanding human motives, behavior, and the context of people's lives, rather than reducing moral life to a simple formula or set of equations.
principles.source_quote: you can't do ethics like mathematics isn't like mathematics it isn't like physics um it's a matter of understanding um what's happening when people make moral judgments

principles.statement: Philosophical writing must be accessible to the reader.
principles.rationale: There is no virtue in writing dully; the entire point of writing is to communicate and get ideas across to people.
principles.source_quote: if I'm going to write something I see no point in doing it if nobody's going to be able to read it

principles.statement: Understanding human action requires understanding motives.
principles.rationale: Behaviorism fails because in actual human life, you must notice and control motives to understand why people do what they do and what vision of life they hold.
principles.source_quote: in the actual conduct of human life On's all the time got to notice motives and to try to control them

mental_models.name: Darwin vs. Newton for Psychology
mental_models.description: Viewing the study of human nature not as a search for abstract, physics-like laws (Newton), but as a detailed, holistic study of how all the complex parts fit together organically (Darwin).
mental_models.example: Applying this to moral philosophy by rejecting the urge to get more and more abstract, and instead studying the details of human behavior and motives.

heuristics: Take a mixed course (philosophy alongside history, English, or psychology) rather than straight philosophy to prevent your thinking from becoming too abstract.

signature_quotes.text: if I'm going to write something I see no point in doing it if nobody's going to be able to read it
signature_quotes.context: On her commitment to writing accessible, clear philosophical prose.

signature_quotes.text: Psychology doesn't didn't need a Newton what it needs is a Darwin so will study all the details and explain why the bits are put together
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing the desire to model psychology and ethics on the abstract laws of physics.

signature_quotes.text: you can't do ethics like mathematics isn't like mathematics it isn't like physics
signature_quotes.context: Explaining what she and her contemporaries (like Anscombe and Foot) agreed upon regarding the flaws of mid-century moral philosophy.

signature_quotes.text: If you try to look at life as a whole or even at your own life you've got to look at all of different directions you've got to um get a panorama haven't you
signature_quotes.context: On the necessity of making connections across disciplines rather than narrowing things down.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores the larger world and bigger questions, making philosophy petty, detailed, and off-putting to the public.
Aiming to simplify moral life to a formula or set of equations ignores the complex reality of human motives and behavior.
It fails to relate philosophical ideas to the hard facts of reality, such as overpopulation or the inevitability of death.

anti_patterns.description: Studying straight philosophy on its own as an undergraduate.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It gets too abstract; it needs to be grounded by mixing it with subjects like history, English, or psychology.
